html:
CR = 2.63
Projection construction with labeled scene and image points.
⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: camera center/pinhole point
image plane rectangle (tiltable) with axis orientation tickmarks
vertical bookshelf segment in 3D, endpoints labeled P4 (top), P2 (bottom), vertical coordinate dimension
true desk height as a horizontal marker/point (P3) on bookshelf, with height arrow labeled h_desk
horizontal ground plane (semi-transparent), blue and grid-labeled
vertical projection lines connecting P4 (top), P3 (desk), and P2 (bottom) straight down onto ground plane
dashed lines from image plane points (t_b, b, g_b, v3) back through camera center to their corresponding 3D points
intersection points on image plane labeled clearly: t_b (top of bookshelf), b (desk), g_b (ground at bookshelf base), v3 (vertical vanishing point, also shown at horizon on image plane)
intersection points on ground plane labeled with match to image plane lines
dimension/arrow brackets labeled h_bookshelf (=value slider), h_desk (value), from P2-P4/P3
collinear alignment marks on the image plane showing t_b, b, g_b, v3 all on the vertical edge
point markers ('dot' or sphere) for each labeled point: P4, P3, P2, t_b, b, g_b, v3
dashed line overlays between each image plane point and its projection down onto the ground plane (along the image vertical edge, to clarify foreshortened distances)
font annotations matching the original diagram for all points and dimension arrows

interactions.label: Desk height (cm)
interactions.teaches: Alters the true height of the desk (P3), moves b along the image edge, and dynamically illustrates how changing height affects both the 3D desk and its perspective-projected position on the bookshelf.

interactions.label: Bookshelf height (cm)
interactions.teaches: Adjusts the real height between P2 and P4 and the corresponding projected t_b and g_b points, showing how different bookshelf heights recalibrate the geometry of all projections but preserve the cross-ratio.

interactions.label: Image tilt (deg)
interactions.teaches: Rotates the image plane, producing visible perspective distortion and foreshortening, vividly demonstrating why simple image distances cannot recover the true heights.

interactions.label: Show cross-ratio annotation
interactions.teaches: Enables dynamic annotation (label and bracket) of the cross-ratio value on the image plane edge and the real 3D object, making the projective invariant visible for any settings.

demo_steps.title: Initial desk/bookshelf scene
demo_steps.narration: Here's our 3D scene: a bookshelf of 197 cm stands on the ground, and the desk is marked at 75 cm up. On the image plane, you see labeled points t_b (top), b (desk), g_b (base), and v3 (vertical vanishing point), all collinear. Dashed rays show how these project from the real scene into the image. Notice the matching brackets for measured heights.

demo_steps.title: Perspective tilt and image distortion
demo_steps.narration: Now, tilt the image plane by 20°. Watch how the projected image points compress or stretch on the image edge: the distance between, say, g_b and t_b no longer matches real heights. This perspective-induced foreshortening shows that raw image distances mislead about true scene sizes.

demo_steps.title: Vary desk height, track projections
demo_steps.narration: Let’s change the desk height to 45 cm. The b point shifts downwards along the image edge. Notice that while image distances vary nonlinearly, the projective layout still rules the correspondence between real height and projected position. Height brackets and arrow labels update in sync on the 3D bookshelf and image.

demo_steps.title: Reveal the cross-ratio: projective invariance
demo_steps.narration: Now enable the cross-ratio annotation. Notice a label on the image edge: even as perspective distorts all distances, the cross-ratio computed from t_b, b, g_b, and v3 stays constant, matching the real 3D ratios. This is the mathematical key that unlocks measurement from a single photo!

demo_steps.title: Scale bookshelf: cross-ratio remains invariant
demo_steps.narration: Finally, set the bookshelf height to 240 cm. Physical heights, and their projections, all change — brackets and image points move — but the cross-ratio value and invariant relationship between real heights and their projected loci are preserved. No matter how the geometry or viewing angle change, cross-ratio allows us to recover true heights!

plan.camera_suggestion: 3D diagonal view slightly above and off to the side, showing both the image plane (partially transparent, labels readable), the 3D vertical bookshelf standing above the ground plane, and all projection lines/rays intersecting at the camera center. Zoom to frame the geometry and ensure all labeled points and connecting lines are visible at all settings.
plan.notes: Dashed lines connect all key image plane points to their corresponding 3D bookshelf/ground locations. Use a consistent label font/size for point names (t_b, b, g_b, v3, P2, P3, P4, etc.) as in the source diagram; render vertical brackets/arrows for heights alongside bookshelf; on toggling cross-ratio, show a dynamic calculation/label (e.g., CR = ...) near the image edge. Highlight (thicken/color) collinear alignments and projections when 'showCrossRatio' is enabled. All dimension arrows/brackets and dashed projections should update reactively.

plan.contextChunk:
$\mathbf{g}_b$ is the projection of the desk height on the bookshelf.
However, due to the foreshortening effect induced by perspective
projection, we cannot simply rely on the ratio of image distances to
estimate the desk's true height. Instead, we will utilize the
cross-ratio invariant to make this estimation.



:::{.column-margin}
As J. J. Gibson pointed out, the ground plane is very
useful for all kinds of 3D scene measurements.
:::

In order to
use the cross-ratio invariant we need two sets of four colinear points
related by a projective geometry. @fig-office_measuring_desk_sketch
shows a sketch of the setup we have in @fig-office_measuring_desk.

![Projection of desk height onto the bookshelf. We start with the bottom and top image coordinates of points along vertical edges of the bookshelf: 
$\mathbf{t}_b = [702~~2{,}958]^\mathsf{T}$ 
$\mathbf{g}_b= [987~~618]^\mathsf{T}$ 
and the desk: 
$\mathbf{t}_d = [679~~1{,}018]^\mathsf{T}$ 
$\mathbf{g}_d= [793~~59]^\mathsf{T}$ 
We compute the points 
$\mathbf{a}= [4{,}471~~2{,}486]^\mathsf{T}$ 
and
$\mathbf{b}= [2{,}237~~2{,}765]^\mathsf{T}$ 
as described in the text.](figures/single_view_3d/office_measuring_desk.png){width="85%" #fig-office_measuring_desk}


In the image plane, we have the four image points $\mathbf{t}_b$,
$\mathbf{b}$, $\mathbf{g}_b$ and $\mathbf{v}_3$. Those four 2D image
points correspond to the 3D locations given by the top of the bookshelf,
desk height, ground and infinity (which is the location of the vertical
vanishing point in 3D).

![Sketch of the setting from @fig-office_measuring_desk. There are two sets of colinear points related by central projection. Their cross-ratios are identical.](figures/single_view_3d/office_measuring_desk_sketch.png){width="70%" #fig-office_measuring_desk_sketch}

The rest of calculations requires computing distances between points,
which only works when using heterogeneous coordinates (or homogeneous
coordinates if the last component is equal to 1 for all points). The
cross-ratio invariant between both sets of four points gives as the
equality:

$$CR(\mathbf{P}_1,\mathbf{P}_2,\mathbf{P}_3,\mathbf{P}_4) = CR(\mathbf{p}_1,\mathbf{p}_2,\mathbf{p}_3,\mathbf{p}_4)
$${#eq-cross_ratio_shelf_desk}

The left side of the equality corresponds to the distances between the
3D points and the right side to the distances of the image points
measured in pixel units. As $\mathbf{P}_1$ is in infinity (as it
corresponds to the vanishing point), the left side ratio only has two
terms, resulting in the ratio between the bookshelf and desk heights. To

elements: camera center / pinhole
tilted image plane rectangle with labeled intersection points
vertical line segment representing the bookshelf with endpoints P2 (bottom) and P4 (top)
vertical line segment representing the desk with endpoints projected onto the bookshelf (b and g_b)
ground plane as blue rectangle
points labeled: P1 (vanishing point of verticals), P2 (base of bookshelf on ground), P3 (desk height point on bookshelf), P4 (top of bookshelf)
dashed projective lines connecting image points (on the image plane) to 3D scene points
arrows or brackets labeled h_bookshelf (=197cm) and h_desk (=?) showing true heights
annotated 2D image points: t_b, g_b, b, v_3 (colinear on image plane)
dashed vertical from P1 at infinity to ground plane

interactions.label: Desk height (cm)
interactions.teaches: Shows how the position of the desk's height (P3) on the bookshelf and its image point (b) changes with different desk heights, illustrating the foreshortening effect and how cross-ratio ties real scene heights to image locations.

interactions.label: Bookshelf height (cm)
interactions.teaches: Adjusts the real height of the bookshelf, demonstrating how all projected points and ratios realign, and how cross-ratios remain invariant under different scene scales.

interactions.label: Image plane tilt (deg)
interactions.teaches: Rotates the image plane to show how perspective projection distorts vertical measurements, emphasizing why simple pixel ratios do not reveal real-world heights.

demo_steps.title: Initial measurement setup
demo_steps.narration: Let’s start by setting the bookshelf height to 197 cm, and the desk height to 75 cm. The image plane is upright. You see the top and base of the bookshelf line up with points t_b and g_b in the image. The projected point b marks the desk height on the image of the bookshelf, but its pixel location is affected by perspective.

demo_steps.title: Perspective foreshortening demo
demo_steps.narration: As you tilt the image plane by 25 degrees, watch how the projections of both the bookshelf top, the desk height, and the ground all compress or stretch. The ratios of image distances change nonlinearly. This visually demonstrates why naïve pixel measurement does not yield real heights — but the cross-ratio is preserved.

demo_steps.title: Estimating desk height: cross-ratio insight
demo_steps.narration: Now, let’s change the actual desk height to 50 cm. See how the b point on the projected bookshelf shifts downward on the image plane, compressing toward the ground. Still, if you compute the cross-ratio between these four collinear points, it is unchanged: it encodes the true height ratio regardless of projection or image tilt.

demo_steps.title: Changing bookshelf size, same cross-ratio
demo_steps.narration: Try increasing the bookshelf height to 240 cm while keeping the desk at 50 cm. The projected points all move, but the relative position of the desk height on the bookshelf image is determined by the same cross-ratio calculation — so you can still solve for unknown scene heights using image measurements!

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: 3D diagonal view that shows the image plane (tilted out of screen) and the vertical bookshelf line, with the ground plane beneath. Ensure the vanishing point for verticals and the projection rays are clearly visible, with the image plane taking up the center foreground.
interactionPlan.notes: Use clear, thick lines for the bookshelf and the projection rays. Vertical vanishing point line (P1) should be rendered distinctly, and dashed lines for all central projections. Points on the image plane (t_b, g_b, b, v_3) should be annotated in both 2D (in the plane) and their corresponding 3D rays. Make bracket/arrows for h_bookshelf and h_desk update interactively. If possible, label cross-ratio values dynamically next to the projected lines.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The image plane is a quadrilateral not properly reflecting the source orientation.
Vertical lines for P2 to P4 are missing dashed lines.
The vanishing point connections do not match the source.
Height brackets are not labeled or positioned similarly to source.
Labels use different fonts and sizing compared to source.
evaluation.notes: The figure captures the main concept but has issues with depth, scale, and labeling.
action_items: Adjust the orientation of the image plane to match the source's quadrilateral shape.
Ensure dashed lines are visible for all rays from pinhole to P2, P3, and P4.
Correct the vanishing point connections and ensure height brackets match the source layout.
Modify the labels to use the same font and size as in the source image.

feedback.rationale: The main concept is captured, but adjustments needed for depth, scale, and labeling suggest generator execution fixes rather than a change in the conceptual plan.
action_items: Adjust the orientation of the image plane to match the source's quadrilateral shape.
Ensure dashed lines are visible for all rays from pinhole to P2, P3, and P4.
Correct the vanishing point connections and ensure height brackets match the source layout.
Modify the labels to use the same font and size as in the source image.
actionItems: Adjust the orientation of the image plane to match the source's quadrilateral shape.
Ensure dashed lines are visible for all rays from pinhole to P2, P3, and P4.
Correct the vanishing point connections and ensure height brackets match the source layout.
Modify the labels to use the same font and size as in the source image.
